gettingStarted.title: Getting started with Cyjan IDS
gettingStarted.subtitle: What to do after a fresh install — in the right order.
gettingStarted.intro: The steps build on each other: without known networks the ML engine cannot tell internal from external traffic apart. Without a DNS resolver allowlist the resolver's replies look like portscans. Without a few days of runtime the ML engine has no baseline to anchor on. Plan for one to two weeks of mutual learning at the start.
gettingStarted.pipelineTitle: The data flow in one sentence
gettingStarted.pipelineNote: Packets from the mirror port → sniffer → flow aggregator → in parallel signature engine (heuristics) + ml engine (IsolationForest) → alert manager (dedup, suppression, severity normalisation) → DB + WebSocket push to the frontend.

networks.title: Maintain known networks and hosts
networks.description: The known-networks table is the anchor for every downstream evaluation. It tells the system which IP ranges belong to your lab/OT/office. The internal/external split for the ML engine, the egress boundary detection and the suppression heuristics all derive from it.
bullets: iTop sync (if available): under Settings → iTop integration set endpoint + API key — the sync pulls all subnets + assets as known networks and host display names.
CSV import: in the Networks tab upload a CIDR CSV.
Manually: add individual subnets via + New. Fine for small labs.
Hosts on top — gives every IP a friendly display name so alerts show web-prod-01 instead of 192.168.1.42.
Consequence: without this list the ML engine treats everything as one network and the rule-tuner cannot learn internal-specific thresholds.

resolver.title: Tell the appliance about your DNS resolver
resolver.description: In the mirror stream the sniffer sees the DNS resolver as the source of many outgoing replies — from a heuristic point of view that looks like a port scan or DNS amplification. Once you list the resolver, its medium/low alerts get filtered; high/critical (real tunnel/DGA hits) still come through.
bullets: Settings → Networks → DNS resolver allowlist: enter the IP of the internal DNS server (e.g. 192.168.1.100).
Multiple allowed — one per line.
Mandatory in OT environments with layer-2/3 DNS forwarders — otherwise you get permanent background noise.

ml.title: Get to know the three ML components and let them learn
ml.description: Cyjan IDS uses three ML components in parallel — each with a different job. They do not work right out of the box: all three need time to learn your baseline.
bullets: (a) Anomaly engine — IsolationForest: sees every flow on its own and scores deviations from the training average. Lifecycle: phase "passthrough" until 500 flows are collected, then "learning" (bootstrap training), then "active". Default threshold 0.65 — recommended on most setups: drop it via Settings → ML status to 0.57 (P99 of typical idle distribution).

(b) rule-tuner: learns the concrete thresholds for every heuristic (SCAN_001, RECON_002, …) from your traffic. Collects metric values per rule and writes adapted overrides after each tuning cycle (default 6 h). Recommended: trigger one manual training (e.g. 2 h) during normal load — afterwards the tuner has your baseline and self-adjusts continuously.

(c) Suppression — adaptive: the alert manager learns the typical hourly rate per (rule_id, ip_pair) over 14 days. If the current hour is in range (Z-score < 2.0) → alert is downgraded to severity=low with tag ml-suppressed. On a real spike (Z ≥ 2.0) the system breaks through suppression automatically. No initial config needed — just mark obvious FPs as "False Positive" in the alert detail drawer, the system does the rest.

dashboard.title: Understand the dashboard and its filters
dashboard.description: The dashboard filter bar combines several independent filters — they all apply together (logical AND).
bullets: Time range: Live (WebSocket stream of fresh alerts) or one of the historic windows (1m / 15m / 1h / 4h / 1d / 2d / 7d) or Custom for a freely chosen range.
Severity pills (critical / high / medium / low): multiple selectable. None selected = show all.
Source: signature (heuristics), ml (IsolationForest), suricata (external rule sets), external (IRMA asset enrichment).
Feedback: only unmarked, only TP-marked, or only FP-marked.
Tap: in multi-tap setups pick a single tap or only master-local alerts.
Show test alerts: synthetic traffic from the Tests tab produces alerts with is_test=true. Hidden by default, toggle here.
ML only: quick filter on source=ml.
IRMA filter Warnings: hides ASSET::* enrichment alerts.
Egress on / on+wl: shows only alerts with a boundary priority. "on+wl" includes whitelisted connections for audit purposes.
Sort: by priority brings boundary P0..P3 to the top instead of pure timestamp ordering.
Group: collapse same rule + source IP — much quieter at the start.
CSV export: export the current filter selection up to 5 000 alerts.

boundary.title: Configure the OT boundary
boundary.description: The egress boundary detects connections out of a defined OT/internal network — and prioritises them from P0 (critical, unusual) to P3 (audit). Whitelisted destinations (defined CIDR + port + proto) are downgraded to P3.
bullets: Prerequisite: known networks from step 1 are filled in — they define what "internal" means.
Settings → Egress boundary: maintain whitelist rules (source/dest/port/proto, optional description).
In the dashboard switch Egress on for the audit view — you see only alerts with a boundary priority, ordered by severity.
Egress on+wl additionally shows whitelisted hits — useful for "who's calling where, even if allowed" reviews.

updates.title: Apply updates
updates.description: Cyjan IDS has two update paths: offline update for running installations (web UI) and ISO for fresh installs.
bullets: Releases live at github.com/JxxKal/ids/releases. Each major tag (e.g. v2.0.0) ships master ISO, tap ISO and update ZIP.

Apply update ZIP: Settings → System update → upload cyjan-ids-update-vX.Y.Z.zip. The backend pulls all container images and recreates all services with --force-recreate. Typically 1–3 min, then the pipeline runs on the new version.
Fresh install: make cyjan-ids-latest.iso or cyjan-ids-tap-latest.iso bootable, install, the setup wizard handles the rest.
Heuristic rule updates (signature-engine YAML rules) need no update package — they ship in the image and hot-reload via inotify.

gettingStarted.footer: Full project docs in README.md. Concrete lab report (pentest against the IDS itself, all 6 phases): docs/cyjankali/lab.html.

topbar.helpOff: Turn off help mode (or press ESC)

dashboard.sidebarNav: Main navigation. Switches between overview, getting-started guide, network inventory, host inventory, test scenarios, and settings.
dashboard.severityPills: Severity filter (multi-select): click toggles a level. None selected = show all. Replaces the previous single-select dropdown.
dashboard.filterSource: Alert source: signature (heuristic rules), ml (IsolationForest anomaly), suricata (external rule sets) or external (IRMA asset enrichment).
dashboard.filterFeedback: User markings: 'no feedback' = not yet rated, FP = false positive (feeds the ML suppression learner), TP = true positive (constrains the rule-tuner).
dashboard.filterTap: For multi-tap setups: pick a single tap or only master-local alerts (tap_id IS NULL). Filter is applied server-side — works for large time windows too.
dashboard.irmaFilterToggle: Hides ASSET::* enrichment alerts from the IRMA source. Useful when IRMA emits many asset status notes that clutter the detection picture.
dashboard.customRange: Freely chosen time window: start/end as datetime-local inputs (browser local timezone). Apply reloads the alerts. Editing does nothing until you press Apply.
dashboard.topbarLive: Live status of the WebSocket connection. Green = real-time alerts streaming, red = no connection to the backend.
dashboard.topbarKpi: Key metrics for the selected time window: total, critical+high, alerts per hour, and visible after filtering.
dashboard.topbarLogout: End the current session and return to the login screen.
dashboard.threatGauge: Live threat level computed from the severity and frequency of recent alerts. Calm = green, active escalation = red.
dashboard.severityCard: Distribution of visible alerts by severity (critical/high/medium/low). Click to filter the list, click again to clear the filter.
dashboard.protocolsCard: Top protocols across visible alerts (TCP ports, UDP services). Useful to spot clusters such as many DNS or RDP alerts at a glance.
dashboard.timeWindow: Display window. 'Live' streams new alerts continuously, the others load a snapshot of the last 1 min / 15 min / 1 h / 4 h / 1 day. Click the active window again to reload it.
dashboard.alertCount: Number of alerts currently visible in the list (after filters and search).
dashboard.unknownHosts: Unknown hosts on the home network that have not been named or tagged yet. Click to open a drawer to maintain names, tags, and notes.
dashboard.snifferDrops: Sniffer packet drops over the last seconds. >1 % = yellow, >5 % = red. Typical causes: high pps or undersized ring buffer.
dashboard.mlOnly: Filter: show only ML engine alerts (IsolationForest anomalies). Signature engine and Suricata alerts are hidden.
dashboard.showTest: Include test alerts. Hidden by default because the test scenarios page can produce hundreds.
dashboard.showSuppressed: Include suppressed alerts (tags 'ml-suppressed'/'auto-suppressed'). These were auto- or ML-tuner-downgraded to 'low' and are hidden by default to de-noise the list.
dashboard.snapshotHint: Note: a snapshot is shown right now. New alerts appear only after reloading or switching back to 'Live'.
dashboard.alertSearch: Full-text search across rule ID, description, tags, IPs, hostnames, and CMDB names. Comma-separated terms are AND-combined.
dashboard.alertEgress: OT-Boundary filter. 'Off' = all alerts, 'On' = traffic crossing the OT boundary with boundary classification only, 'On+WL' = also include already whitelisted connections.
dashboard.alertSortPriority: Sort by OT-Boundary priority (P0 first) instead of time. Off = newest first.
dashboard.alertGroup: Groups identical alerts (same rule + same endpoints) into one row with a counter, instead of listing every repetition separately.
dashboard.alertRow: An alert row: click to open the detail view with PCAP preview, connection graph, and true/false-positive feedback.
dashboard.boundaryCell: OT-Boundary classification for traffic crossing the OT boundary. Three pills: N = destination network known (CMDB entry), S = source host known (trust list), D = destination host known. Priority follows: P0 = nothing known (highest), P1 = only one endpoint known, P2 = both endpoints or network known, P3 = network + one endpoint known. All three known = no boundary alert. ·WL = already whitelisted.

alertFeed.whitelistRowAction: Whitelist this flow as legitimate egress

whitelistModal.title: Egress whitelist entry
whitelistModal.reasonPlaceholder: e.g. Deliberate outbound sync to HQ VPN endpoint
whitelistModal.noSrcIp: Alert has no source IP – no whitelist match possible.

pcap.unavailable: No PCAP – sniffer not running or no packet buffer for this alert

feedback.fpTitle: False positive – confirmed, feeds into ML training
feedback.tpTitle: True positive – attack confirmed, feeds into ML training

remoteTaps.title: Remote Taps

pending.title: Pending auto-pair requests ({{count}})
pending.subtitle: approve or reject — afterwards the tap can fetch its mTLS cert

remoteTaps.intro: Distributed capture nodes that ship their alerts to this master over mTLS WSS. Generate a pairing token per tap, hand it to the tap wizard (together with this master's API URL), and the tap pairs itself automatically. The token is one-shot and valid for 60 minutes – it is shown here only once, copy it immediately.
remoteTaps.tokenInstruction: Copy this token now and paste it into the tap wizard. It will not be displayed again. Master API URL for the tap wizard: {{url}}
remoteTaps.revokeConfirmBody: Tap '{{name}}' will be disconnected immediately and cannot reconnect. This is irreversible – a new pairing token plus a new tap ID would be required to re-pair.

egressPriorities.title: Egress priorities
egressPriorities.intro: Mapping from the classification tuple (network known? source known? destination known?) to a priority P0–P3 used to sort the AlertFeed egress filter. The table shows all 7 egress-relevant tuples (the eighth – ✓✓✓ – falls out of the egress view by definition). Persisted in system_config key 'boundary_priority_map'; the enrichment-service picks it up within 60 s without restart.
egressPriorities.applyHint: Changes apply only to new alerts from save time onwards; existing records are not retroactively reclassified.

tuples.100: Rogue device in managed network heading out

dnsResolvers.title: DNS resolver allowlist
dnsResolvers.intro: Enter the IPs (or CIDR ranges) of your DNS resolvers here. Alerts with severity medium or low that involve one of these resolvers as source or destination are dropped before being written to the database. High and critical alerts (e.g. DNS tunnelling, DGA) remain visible.
dnsResolvers.rationale: Background: the sniffer sees two unidirectional flows per DNS conversation. The server-response flow (resolver → client:ephemeral) looks like a port scan because the resolver answers many distinct ephemeral ports within 60 s. The allowlist hides these FPs.
dnsResolvers.noEntries: No resolvers configured – filter is inactive.
dnsResolvers.applyHint: Changes take effect within 60 seconds without restarting the alert-manager.

general.title: Language & display
general.languageHelp: Language is stored in this browser and applies to this device only.

users.title: Users
users.tokenSaveHint: Store the token securely – it cannot be retrieved again.
users.apiUserHint: API users use long-lived JWT tokens (365 days) instead of passwords. The token is shown once after creation.
users.tokenButtonTitle: Generate new API token (does not invalidate the previous one)

saml.importIdpHint: Download the XML from FortiAuthenticator and paste it here — fills the IdP fields automatically.
saml.idpCert: IdP X.509 certificate (Base64, no PEM header)
saml.spHeading: Service Provider (SP) – enter these values in FortiAuthenticator
saml.attrMappingHint: Attribute mapping — name of the SAML assertion attribute that delivers each value. FortiAuthenticator sends e.g. username or uid depending on configuration.
saml.defaultRole: Default role for new SAML users

mlStatus.trainingNowSub: Quantile calculation + IsolationForest fit. Typical duration 30 s to 5 min depending on sample count.
mlStatus.retrainNowHint: Triggers an immediate retrain (overrides the interval once). Model is refitted with current flow data + manually marked samples.
mlStatus.title: AI/ML engine
mlStatus.bannerPassthrough: The model is waiting for sufficient network data. ML filtering is not active yet – all flows pass through.
mlStatus.bannerLearning: The model has been initially trained and is being refined continuously. ML filtering is already active but not yet fully calibrated.
mlStatus.bannerActive: The model is fully trained. Anomalous traffic is detected automatically and flagged as ML alerts.
mlStatus.featureDeviationsTitle: Why are flows flagged as anomalous?
mlStatus.featureDeviationsSubtitle: (factor: how much higher/lower the feature is in alerted flows vs. normal flows)
mlStatus.deviationTooltip: Average of this feature in ML alerts vs. normal flows. Raw deviation: {{pct}} %. For large spreads we show the factor (e.g. 91× = 9 100 %).
mlStatus.noAlerts24h: No ML alerts in the last 24 hours – no anomalous traffic detected, or ML filter configured too leniently.

alertThreshold.label: Alert threshold
alertThreshold.hint: Score above which a flow counts as an anomaly (0.50–0.95). Higher = fewer but more confident alerts. For large environments with lots of background noise, recommended: 0.75–0.85.

contamination.hint: Expected share of anomalous flows in the training data (0.1 %–20 %). Changing this triggers an automatic retrain. OT/SCADA with stable traffic: 0.5–1 %. Large IT networks: 2–5 %.

bootstrap.label: Minimum flows for training
bootstrap.hint: Number of flows that must be collected before the first model is trained. For large networks with broad traffic profile: 2,000–10,000.

partialFit.label: Scaler update interval
partialFit.hint: How often the feature normaliser (StandardScaler) is updated incrementally. Smaller value = faster adaptation to new traffic patterns, higher CPU load.

retrainInterval.label: Auto re-training interval
retrainInterval.hint: How often the training-loop triggers a full retrain of the IsolationForest model. Default 24 h. Visible in the ML status section (Last training / Next scheduled).

hourly.label: Hourly
hourly.desc: Aggressive adaptation — only useful in test setups

mlLearned.title: Adaptive ML suppression
mlLearned.intro1: Per (rule_id + source/destination pair), an hourly baseline (mean + StdDev) is learned from the last
mlLearned.intro2: Suppression only kicks in when the current rate is statistically inconspicuous
mlLearned.spikeIntro1: Spikes against the baseline break suppression automatically — the pattern stays learned, but the burst becomes visible again because it is NO longer normal.
mlLearned.spikeIntro2: are also lifted on a spike: a connection previously marked FP can later become an attack path (C2, exfil) — a sudden surge must reach the analyst. TP feedback removes the pattern immediately.
mlLearned.noBaselinesHint: Appear automatically after at least {{hours}} h of data points per pattern
mlLearned.sourceColTitle: manual = user marked FP · learned = automatically learned
mlLearned.baselineColTitle: Baseline: mean alerts per hour ± StdDev
mlLearned.dataColTitle: Hours where the pattern was observed so far
mlLearned.manualTitle: User marked an alert of this connection as FP
mlLearned.refreshFooter: Alert manager refresh: 60 s · UI refresh: 30 s · ENV: SUPPRESSION_LEARN_WINDOW_D, SUPPRESSION_MIN_HOURS, SUPPRESSION_Z_THRESHOLD

mlOverview.title: AI / ML — overview
mlOverview.intro: Three learning components run in parallel and complement each other. This page shows their status at a glance — detailed configuration via the quick links.

anomaly.title: Anomaly engine
anomaly.subtitle: IsolationForest
anomaly.description: Generates new alerts (source=ml) for flows that deviate from normal behaviour.

tuner.title: Threshold tuner
tuner.subtitle: Reservoir + quantile
tuner.description: Adjusts thresholds of tunable heuristic rules to the network's distribution. Preserves severity instead of degrading it.

suppression.title: Pattern suppression
suppression.subtitle: Layer 1 + 2
suppression.description: Throttles FP patterns per IP pair down to low. Skipped for ML-engine output and tunable heuristics (rule-tuner is responsible there).

ruleOverrides.title: Rule adjustments
ruleOverrides.intro: Per signature-engine rule you can tune thresholds, override the severity, or disable the rule entirely. If a heuristic floods you with normal traffic, raise the threshold — that's usually cleaner than downgrading severity, because real hits never get suppressed in the first place. Adjustments live in /sig-rules/custom/_overrides.json and are picked up within seconds, without a restart.
ruleOverrides.scopeNote: This page lists only the signature-engine YAML rules ({{count}} total) – the flow-based heuristics such as SCAN_002, DNS_AMP_001, ANOMALY_FRAGMENT_001, etc. The much larger collection of Suricata rules (thousands, .rules format) lives under Rule set → Active rules and Custom signatures and is managed there separately.
ruleOverrides.resetAllConfirm: Really reset all overrides? YAML defaults will take effect again.
ruleOverrides.thresholdsHint: Values apply only to this rule. Fields you don't change keep the YAML default.
ruleOverrides.applyHint: Changes take effect within ~5 seconds without restarting the service.

badges.mlTooltip: Set by rule-tuner — manual edit overrides with source=manual
badges.manualTooltip: Manually set — rule-tuner won't touch this param
badges.rowMlTooltip: {{count}} param(s) set by rule-tuner — expand to see values
badges.rowManualTooltip: {{count}} param(s) manually set — tuner respects them

mlTuning.title: ML tuning
mlTuning.intro: The rule-tuner samples distributions per rule+param and sets thresholds automatically once training ends. Manually-edited values (scalar form below) are left alone.

rules.updateRequested: Update requested at {{ts}} – Suricata is reloading…
rules.offlineImportHint1: For machines without internet: upload an ETOpen tarball or your own
rules.offlineImportHint2: file. The file lands in the snort-rules volume and Suricata receives a
rules.editorIntro: Editor for your own *.rules files in the snort-rules volume. On save, suricata -T runs server-side against the entire rules stack – syntax errors are rejected, live reload via SIGUSR2 is transparent. Built-in files (pulled from URL sources) are read-only to protect against being overwritten by the next update.
rules.selectFileHint: Select a file on the left, or click „+ New file" above.
rules.newFileHeader2: automatically runs against the whole rules stack.
rules.builtinReadonly: Built-in file – read only. Put custom changes in a separate file so the next update does not overwrite them.
rules.noRulesLoaded: No rules loaded – please start an update.
rules.overrideHint: Severity override and active toggle apply per Suricata SID to all incoming alerts. snort-bridge applies them BEFORE the alert is published to Kafka – so a 'low' override doesn't suppress, it only re-grades; a disabled SID drops the alert entirely. Persisted in /sig-rules/custom/_suricata_overrides.json, ~30 s reload.

dbMaint.title: Database maintenance
dbMaint.intro: Destructive actions also require the admin password and are recorded in the audit log.
dbMaint.cleanupHint: Deletes records by criteria. Recorded in the audit log.
dbMaint.factoryResetWarn2: alerts, flows, training samples, test runs. Config and users remain. To confirm, type "DELETE CYJAN":
dbMaint.vacuumHint: Reclaims dead rows and updates statistics. FULL rewrites the table completely and locks it — only when needed.
dbMaint.retentionHint: Old data can be deleted automatically by hypertable policy. Currently active: {{active}}.
dbMaint.retentionHint2: Retention per hypertable. Without a policy the data grows unbounded — click a row to prefill, then set or remove the period.
dbMaint.emergencyOn: active. If the disk exceeds {{trigger}}% despite retention, the oldest data is deleted automatically until {{target}}% is reached (order with protected floor: {{tables}}). Currently {{disk}}% used.
dbMaint.emergencyOff: disabled. No automatic cleanup when the disk fills up.
dbMaint.backupHint1: Full pg_dump as gzip. The dump contains schema + data and can be restored in place using the restore button.

versionInfo.title: Version info
versionInfo.subtitle: Full version history. Use the buttons above to jump straight to a version.

ssl.title: SSL / TLS certificate
ssl.serverHostnameHint: hostname or IP the web server listens on. Takes effect on next container restart.
ssl.hostnameSaved: Hostname saved – nginx active on next restart ✓
ssl.pemHint: Upload an existing PEM certificate and the corresponding private key.
ssl.pfxHint: Imports a PFX/PKCS#12 file (e.g. exported from a Windows CA). Certificate and private key are extracted automatically.
ssl.pfxPasswordPlaceholder: Leave empty if no password is set
ssl.selfSignedHint: Generates a self-signed certificate on the server. Suitable for internal deployments without a public domain.
ssl.acmeHint: Automatic certificate issuance via ACME (e.g. Let's Encrypt). The server must be reachable on ports 80/443.
ssl.emailDomainRequired: E-mail and at least one domain are required

syslog.title: Syslog / SIEM export
syslog.intro: Forwards new alerts to a syslog server every 30 seconds. Supports RFC 5424, CEF (ArcSight, QRadar) and LEEF (IBM QRadar).
syslog.enterHostFirst: Please enter a syslog host first

itop.title: iTop CMDB integration
itop.intro3: from an iTop instance. Hosts get
itop.baseUrlHint: Base URL of the iTop instance (without /webservices/rest.php).
itop.orgFilterHint: Exact iTop organisation name – filters subnets and CIs to one org.

irma.title: IRMA integration
irma.savedOk: Saved ✓ – the IRMA bridge picks up the changes automatically on the next poll.
irma.intro1: Polls the REST API of an IRMA IDS appliance and imports its alarms as an
irma.intro2: source into the Cyjan dashboard. Credential changes apply automatically on the next poll cycle – no restart needed.
irma.baseUrlHint: Full URL incl. scheme and /rest path.

interfaces.title: Network interfaces
interfaces.confirmSniffer: Set sniffer interface to {{iface}} and restart sniffer?
interfaces.confirmMgmt: Set management interface to {{iface}} (stack restart required)?
interfaces.snifferSwitched: Sniffer is being switched to {{iface}} – brief interruption (~5 s).
interfaces.mgmtSaved: Management interface saved on {{iface}}. Stack restart required.
interfaces.footerHint: Sniffer: interface is brought up automatically on start (ip link set up). Management interface changes require a stack restart.

update.title: System update
update.intro1: Download the latest version as a ZIP from GitHub and import it here. Configuration (
update.forceTitle: Re-applies the bundle despite the version check — e.g. after image drift or deliberate re-install of the current build
update.restartHint: Restarts all services (~20 s interruption). Configuration and data are preserved.
update.apiRestarting: API container is restarting — please wait (~15 s) …

systemHealth.title: System Details
systemHealth.dropCritBanner: ⚠ Critical packet loss on the sniffer interface
systemHealth.dropWarnBanner: ⚠ Elevated packet loss on the sniffer interface
systemHealth.dropRateMsg: Drop rate {{pct}} % — possible causes: mirror port overloaded, sniffer CPU too slow, or Kafka backpressure. Packets are missing from analysis.
